speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. Thanks for seeing me today.

speaker: doctor
text: Hello! Good to see you. How have you been feeling since your last visit?

speaker: patient
text: Honestly, a bit off—I’ve had some dizziness in the mornings and my energy’s been low.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your vitals. Your diastolic blood pressure today is 90 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: That sounds high—I thought it was supposed to be under 80?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes, and your systolic reading is 129 mmHg—so overall, 129 over 90.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Insulin glargine 16 units at night. We’ll keep that for now, but let’s review your glucose control closely.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been really fatigued lately—even after a full night’s sleep.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your consistent elevated diastolic pressure and family history, I’m diagnosing essential hypertension.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose came back at 9.5 mmol/L—higher than our target range.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been trying to watch my carbs, but maybe I’m not doing enough?

speaker: doctor
text: I’m going to start you on Atorvastatin 40 mg at night—not just for cholesterol, but also for vascular protection given your hypertension and glucose levels.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll take that as prescribed. Should I monitor anything specific?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—please check your blood glucose before breakfast and at bedtime for the next week. Let me know if you see patterns.

speaker: doctor
text: Good news: your follow-up glucose today is 7.2 mmol/L—much improved from the 9.5 we saw earlier this morning.

speaker: patient
text: That’s reassuring. I did skip dessert last night and walked for 20 minutes after dinner.

speaker: doctor
text: That likely helped. Let’s also recheck your blood pressure in a few minutes to confirm consistency.

speaker: patient
text: It’s still 90—same as that reading when you first checked.

speaker: doctor
text: Right—the same diastolic number we recorded at the start of the visit. That confirms persistent elevation, so we’ll need to optimize your antihypertensive plan soon.

speaker: patient
text: Understood. Should I schedule a follow-up in two weeks?
